rationale: The citing paper uses McKenzie and Liersch (2011) to motivate a core theoretical expectation: consumers may misread compounded growth and thus undervalue annuity annual increases, especially when expressed as percentages. It then returns to this citation in the results and discussion to interpret its own additive-versus-percentage findings and the effect of cumulative payout tables as consistent with prior evidence on compounding misunderstandings. Because the reference informs hypothesis framing and interpretation of key results (not just background listing), the citation is substantive.
relation_description: The citing paper applies McKenzie and Liersch's compounding-misperception insight to annuity decumulation choices. It tests whether consumers undervalue percentage-based annual increases and whether enriched cumulative payout information reduces that bias in annuity evaluation.
